claims.statement: NK cell dysfunction is a central immune defect in endometriosis: reduced NKG2D activating receptor, increased inhibitory receptors NKG2A, KIR2DL1, LILRB1, and sICAM-1 blocking the LFA-1/ICAM-1 synapse.

Endometriosis is an immune-mediated disease with macrophage M2 polarization, reduced NK cytotoxicity, complement activation (C3a/C5a), Th2 predominance, Treg increase, and B-cell activation with autoantibodies.

Immune profiles differ by endometriosis phenotype: peritoneal (stage-dependent M1→M2), ovarian (local immunosuppression + Treg expansion), adenomyosis (Th17/Treg, HMGB-1/TLR4), deep infiltrating (IDO1/COX-2/MMP-9, PD-1+ NK exhaustion), extragenital (complete NK absence CD56-, maximal TGF-β/PD-L1).
Estrogen suppresses NK cell cytotoxicity via reduced Granzyme B and NKG2D, and induces COX-2+CD16- NK cell differentiation producing IL-10 and TGF-β, promoting immunotolerance.
Protopanaxadiol (ginseng extract, estrogen antagonist) enhances NK cytotoxicity — potential therapeutic agent.
Uterine NK cells constitute 30-40% of leukocytes in proliferative phase and up to 70% in secretory phase; in endometriosis they may contribute to infertility and miscarriage.
Immune checkpoint pathways PD-1/PD-L1, TIM-3/Gal-9, CTLA-4/CD80/CD86, and CD47/SIRPα are dysregulated in endometriosis; anti-PD-L1 and anti-CTLA4 reduce lesion size in animal models.
Endometriosis shares immunopathological similarities with cancer: immune evasion, M2-like macrophage polarization, NK suppression, and immune checkpoint activation.
scRNA-seq with machine learning identified GNLY (granulysin), PRF1 (perforin 1), and ENTPD1 (CD39) as key biomarkers of NK dysfunction; three-gene model AUC = 0.84.
ENTPD1 (CD39) is highly expressed in endometrial stromal cells and enhances migration and invasion while modulating NK cells via adenosine signaling.
HLA-E and HLA-G expressed on endometriotic lesions protect them from NK cell killing by binding inhibitory receptors.

36.89% of endometriosis datasets contain eutopic endometrium ONLY — more than any other biospecimen type; almost half of all biospecimens labeled 'endometriosis' have no representation of actual endometriotic disease.
Using eutopic endometrium as a surrogate for ectopic endometriotic tissue is inappropriate despite similarities — the field has a 'persistent bias in both thought and methodology'.

Only 35% of cells in endometriotic lesions originate from grafted endometrium; 65% are host-derived infiltrating cells (NK cells, B cells, macrophages, T cells, fibroblasts, neutrophils, endothelial cells).
The majority of cells in endometriotic lesions are immune cells, not endometrial cells; 'Endometriosis is clearly a disease of the immune system.'
All currently approved therapies are hormonal, targeting autograft stromal cells which are NOT the majority of cells in the lesion — immunotherapies used in cancer may be applicable.
TET3-overexpressing macrophages are key pathogenic contributors and potential therapeutic targets in endometriosis.

Endometriosis lesions are oligoclonal structures: epithelial cells in lesions consist of unrelated clones, stromal cells are distinct from epithelial clones, and lesions at different body sites have independently derived clone sets.
Mutation burdens, signatures, and driver landscapes are similar in lesions and normal endometrium; clonal ancestors of endometriosis are found in normal endometrium of patients.
Endometriosis in males is extremely rare — only ~17-20 documented cases in literature as of 2024.
The main risk factor for male endometriosis is prolonged estrogen therapy for prostate carcinoma; other factors include cirrhosis, spironolactone use, and persistent Müllerian duct syndrome.
Müllerian duct remnant metaplasia is the most accepted pathogenesis theory for male endometriosis — embryologic cell rests along the route of Müllerian ducts.
Complete surgical excision is curative for male endometriosis with no recurrence reported.
Pooled prevalence of endometriosis in transgender men is 25.14% (CI: 17.24-33.94%) — higher than the general cisgender female population (~10%).
Testosterone therapy does NOT fully suppress endometriosis — the disease can persist despite masculinizing hormone therapy, challenging the notion that endometriosis is purely estrogen-driven.
70.58% of transgender men using testosterone reported dysmenorrhea; 21.9% of patients without pelvic pain still had endometriosis.

Multi-omics of ovarian endometriomas (scRNA-seq + spatial transcriptomics + MALDI-MSI metabolomics) identified XBP1, VCAN, and CLDN7 as key epithelial markers, with Claudin family (CLDN3, CLDN4, CLDN7) as core targets.
PAEP, MMP7, and MUC5B are upregulated in epithelial cells of ovarian endometriomas, supporting the implantation theory of endometriosis.
Spatial metabolomics revealed altered cytochrome P450 enzymes, lipoprotein particles, and cholesterol metabolism in mesenchymal regions of ovarian endometriomas.
CIBERSORTx deconvolution identified 5 major cell types and 52 distinct cell subtypes; MUC5B+ epithelial cells and dStromal-late mesenchymal cells are dual drivers of fibrosis and inflammation.
A random forest model based on cell proportions achieved AUC = 0.932 for endometriosis diagnosis; MUC5B+ epithelial cells were the top predictive feature.
Most epithelial subtypes (SOX9_I, SOX9_f_II, Gla_s, Cil) are DECREASED in endometriosis while MUC5B+, Lum, and KRT subtypes are INCREASED; eM2 macrophages are elevated in stage III-IV.
The human omentum provides molecular cues for trans-coelomic spread: omental conditioned media promotes cell migration in both endometriosis and ovarian cancer primary cultures.
Omental conditioned media contains high levels of HGF, SDF-1α, MCP-1, VEGF-A, IL-6, and IL-8; HGF/c-MET signaling mediates cell migration in both conditions.
c-MET inhibition (PHA665752) significantly reduces cell migration — a potential therapeutic target for endometriosis.
PTTG1 is consistently upregulated in migrated cells of both endometriosis and ovarian cancer.
Omentectomy during endometriosis surgery is proposed as a clinical trial concept; endometriosis is benign but shares distribution pattern with ovarian cancer metastasis.
Whole-genome methylation profiling of menstrual stem cells (MenSCs) via EM-seq identified DMRs enriched in genes linked to inflammation, tissue remodeling, and development.
Machine learning model based on MenSC DNA methylation achieves specificity 83% and sensitivity 79% for endometriosis diagnosis — a promising non-invasive approach.
DMRs in MenSCs map to WNT signaling, stem cell regulation, and extracellular matrix remodeling pathways.
Integration of MenSC methylation data with scRNA-seq (HECA dataset) showed methylation changes correlate with transcriptional suppression in MenSC-like stromal populations.
Genes ZNF516, TPGS1, GPM6B, CLMP, and PPFIA1 are differentially methylated and downregulated transcriptionally in endometriosis MenSCs.

The Human Endometrial Cell Atlas (HECA) is a high-resolution single-cell reference of 313,527 cells from 63 women with and without endometriosis, validated with 312,246 nuclei from 63 additional donors.
SOX9+ basalis (CDH2+) cells are putative epithelial stem/progenitor cells, mapped to basalis glands via spatial transcriptomics and smFISH.
GWAS integration pinpoints decidualized stromal cells (dStromal) and macrophages as the most likely dysregulated cell types in endometriosis; no endometriosis-specific cell state was found.
MUC5B+ cells are identified as likely endocervical contamination rather than true endometrial cells.
New cell types discovered: SOX9+ basalis stem/progenitors, PreGlandular and PreLuminal transitional cells, eStromal MMPs, and dStromal early/mid/late decidualized populations.
Functionalis layer features dynamic TGFβ signaling with spatial boundaries maintained by LEFTY1/LEFTY2 antagonists; basalis niche uses CXCR4/CXCL12, WNT (RSPO1/LGR4), and FGF signaling.
Gut dysbiosis in endometriosis: increased Firmicutes/Bacteroidetes ratio, increased Shigella/E. coli, decreased Clostridia; vaginal: decreased Lactobacillus, increased Gardnerella and Atopobium.
LPS from Gram-negative bacteria activates TLR4, promoting pro-inflammatory cytokines, angiogenesis, and lesion growth in endometriosis.
Gut bacteria produce β-glucuronidase, deconjugating estrogens and increasing the pool of active estrogen — the 'estrobolome' mechanism linking microbiota to hyperestrogenic state in endometriosis.

Microbiota contributes to endometriosis through five mechanisms: inflammatory response, immune dysregulation (NK/T-cell via PD-1/PD-L1), estrogen metabolism (estrobolome), lipid metabolism (SCFA depletion), and the brain-gut axis.
The microbiome offers potential as early non-invasive diagnostic biomarkers: Anaerococcus (vaginal) for staging, Ruminococcus (gut) + Pseudomonas (pelvic) for early diagnosis.
Therapeutic approaches targeting the microbiome include probiotics (Lactobacillus strains), dietary intervention, fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT), antibiotics, and postbiotics (butyrate).
